source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure iptables is installed
description: iptables allows configuration of the IPv4 and IPv6 tables in the linux kernel and the rules stored within them. Most firewall configuration utilities operate as a front end to iptables.
rationale: iptables is required for firewall management and configuration.
audit: Run the following command and verify iptables is installed: grep '\"name\": \"iptables\"' /etc/cos-package-info.json "name": "iptables"
remediation: Update to an OS image that includes the iptables package.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the installation of `iptables` on Container-Optimized OS (COS) requires direct inspection of the instance’s file system, specifically `/etc/cos-package-info.json`. No GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native service (including OS Config, Cloud Asset Inventory, or Monitoring) currently exposes guest-level package inventory for COS. Therefore, automation via GCP APIs is not possible.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target Google Cloud VM instance running Container-Optimized OS: - Use the GCP Console or run: `gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone <ZONE>` 2. On the instance, execute the following command to check for `iptables`: - `grep "\"name\": \"iptables\"" /etc/cos-package-info.json` 3. Review the output: - If the output contains `"name": "iptables"`, then `iptables` is installed. - If there is no output, `iptables` is not installed. 4. Document the result for compliance evidence.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure correct container image is set for stackdriver logging agent
description: stackdriver-logging service runs stackdriver container image to export logs to Cloud Logging. Note: This recommendation is not applicable for COS images using Fluent-bit as it isn't containerized.
rationale: If the logging agent is not set correctly, the logs cannot be exported to Cloud Logging.
audit: Verify LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE is set to a correct stackdriver container image. Use the following command to verify: # grep LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE /etc/stackdriver/env_vars LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE="gcr.io/stackdriver-agents/stackdriver-logging- agent:<version>"
remediation: Edit the LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE variable in the /etc/stackdriver/env_vars file to set the correct logging agent. Run the following command to restart stackdriver-logging service : # systemctl restart stackdriver-logging /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the `LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE` environment variable requires direct inspection of a file (`/etc/stackdriver/env_vars`) inside the guest OS of a Container-Optimized OS instance. This information is not exposed via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`), or GCP CLI, nor is it available through instance metadata or configuration management tools. Therefore, automation through GCP-native tools is not possible.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Container-Optimized OS instance running the stackdriver-logging service. 2. Run the command: `grep LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE /etc/stackdriver/env_vars` 3. Verify that the output matches the expected format: `LOGGING_AGENT_DOCKER_IMAGE="gcr.io/stackdriver-agents/stackdriver-logging-agent:<version>"` 4. Ensure the `<version>` specified is the recommended or latest version provided by Google for the stackdriver logging agent. 5. Confirm compliance by ensuring the variable is set correctly, which is necessary for logs to be exported to Cloud Logging.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Logging Service is Running
description: Some form of logging for the OS should be used. On COS Stackdriver-logging agent or fluent-bit logging agent are provided and can be activated simply by following the guides. It is not require to use these for logging but they are simple to set up and will work well.
rationale: If no logging is running, their will be less of a trail for audit if things go wrong. It is a good practice to have logging enabled for better visibility.
audit: This audit procedure is only if you are using one of the supporting logging services described. Stackdriver-Logging Agent Run the following command to verify stackdriver-logging is running: # systemctl status stackdriver-logging | grep Active Active: active (running) since <Day date time> Fluent-bit Logging Or if your system has fluent-bit, run the following command to verify fluent-bit is running: # systemctl status fluent-bit | grep Active Active: active (running) since <Day date time>
remediation: This remediation is only if you want to use one of the supported logging services described. Stackdriver-logging Agent Run the following command to enable stackdriver-logging : # systemctl start stackdriver-logging Fluent-bit Logging Run the following command to enable fluent-bit : # systemctl start fluent-bit Works for Both Simply update the instance metadata to enable logging as follows: # gcloud compute instances add-metadata <instance-name> \ --zone <compute-zone> \ --metadata google-logging-enabled=true
justification: The control requires verifying that the Stackdriver-logging or fluent-bit agent is actively running on the OS of Container-Optimized OS (COS) instances. There is no GCP API, gcloud command, or Cloud Monitoring/Config rule that can directly check the runtime status of arbitrary systemd services on guest OSes without SSH access. While some indirect or partial automation (such as checking for agent installation or log presence) is possible, these do not satisfy the control's explicit requirement to confirm the service is "active (running)" via systemctl. Therefore, this audit must be performed manually.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the COS Instance:** - Use the following command to access the instance: ``` gcloud compute ssh <instance-name> --zone <compute-zone> ``` 2. **Check Stackdriver-Logging Agent Status:** - Run: ``` systemctl status stackdriver-logging | grep Active ``` - Confirm the output shows: `Active: active (running)` 3. **Check Fluent-bit Logging Agent Status (if applicable):** - Run: ``` systemctl status fluent-bit | grep Active ``` - Confirm the output shows: `Active: active (running)` 4. **Verify Compliance:** - At least one of the logging agents (Stackdriver-logging or fluent-bit) must be active and running. - If neither is running, the instance is non-compliant.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure logging is configured
description: For an image using stackdriver, the /etc/stackdriver/logging.config.d/*.conf files specifies rules for logging and which files are to be used to log certain classes of messages. An image using fluent-bit uses /usr/share/fluent-bit/fluent- bit.conf for the same purpose.
rationale: A great deal of important security-related information is sent via logging (e.g., successful and failed su attempts, failed login attempts, root login attempts, etc.).
audit: Review the contents of the /etc/stackdriver/logging.config.d/*.conf if using an image with stackdriver or /usr/share/fluent-bit/fluent-bit.conf if using an image with fluent-bit to ensure appropriate logging is set. In addition, run the following command and verify that the log files are logging information: # ls -l /var/log/
remediation: Edit the contents of /etc/stackdriver/logging.config.d/*.conf if using an image with stackdriver or /usr/share/fluent-bit/fluent-bit.conf if using an image with fluent-bit as appropriate for your environment. Then run the following commands to reload the logging configuration: For stackdriver-logging: # systemctl restart stackdriver-logging For fluent-bit: # systemctl restart fluent-bit /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above need to be performed after every boot for images using stackdriver. This is not the case for fluent- bit as the logging agent is in /usr/share/ which isn't stateless so changes will be persistent across reboots. References: 1. See the rsyslog.conf(5) man page for more information.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires inspection of OS-level configuration files within the guest OS (specifically, `/etc/stackdriver/logging.config.d/*.conf` or `/usr/share/fluent-bit/fluent-bit.conf`). These files are not accessible via any GCP API, Google Cloud SDK, or GCP CLI. GCP APIs only provide metadata about the instance, not the contents of files inside the VM. Therefore, manual SSH access and inspection are required to verify compliance.
manual_summary: 1. Identify all Compute Engine instances running Container-Optimized OS that are in scope for this control. 2. SSH into each relevant instance using the Google Cloud Console or `gcloud compute ssh <instance-name> --zone=<zone>`. 3. For Stackdriver users: - Run `cat /etc/stackdriver/logging.config.d/*.conf` and review the configuration files to ensure appropriate logging rules are set for security-relevant events. For Fluent Bit users: - Run `cat /usr/share/fluent-bit/fluent-bit.conf` and review the configuration for proper logging directives. 4. Verify that logging is active by running `ls -l /var/log/` and confirming that relevant log files are being generated and updated. 5. Ensure that the logging configuration covers key security events (e.g., authentication attempts, privilege escalations). 6. Document findings and confirm that logging is configured as required by the CIS benchmark.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure journald is configured to compress large log files
description: The journald system includes the capability of compressing overly large files to avoid filling up the system with logs or making the logs unmanageably large.
rationale: Uncompressed large files may unexpectedly fill a filesystem leading to resource unavailability. Compressing logs prior to write can prevent sudden, unexpected filesystem impacts.
audit: Review /etc/systemd/journald.conf and verify that large files will be compressed: # grep -e Compress /etc/systemd/journald.conf # Compress=yes
remediation: Edit the /etc/systemd/journald.conf file and add the following line: Compress=yes Reload the configuration to be effective. # systemctl force-reload systemd-journald /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot. References: 1. https://github.com/konstruktoid/hardening/blob/master/systemd.adoc#etcsystemd journaldconf Additional Information: The main configuration file /etc/systemd/journald.conf is read before any of the custom *.conf files. If there are custom configs present, they override the main configuration parameters
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires direct inspection of the `/etc/systemd/journald.conf` file on the underlying Container-Optimized OS instance. There are no GCP APIs, gcloud commands, or GCP-native automation tools that can access or verify the contents of this OS-level configuration file. The only feasible method is to SSH into the instance and manually check the configuration, as this setting is not exposed or manageable via GCP's cloud management interfaces.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target Container-Optimized OS instance using the command: `gcloud compute ssh <instance-name> --zone=<zone>` 2. Run the following command to check the journald configuration: `grep -e Compress /etc/systemd/journald.conf` 3. Verify that the output contains `Compress=yes`. 4. If `Compress=yes` is present, the instance is compliant. If not, remediation is required. 5. Note: Because `/etc` is stateless on Container-Optimized OS, this check must be performed after every boot.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure journald is configured to write logfiles to persistent disk
description: Data from journald may be stored in volatile memory or persisted locally on the server. Logs in memory will be lost upon a system reboot. By persisting logs to local disk on the server they are protected from loss.
rationale: Writing log data to disk will provide the ability to forensically reconstruct events which may have impacted the operations or security of a system even after a system crash or reboot.
audit: Review /etc/systemd/journald.conf and verify that logs are persisted to disk: # grep -e Storage /etc/systemd/journald.conf # Storage=persistent
remediation: Edit the /etc/systemd/journald.conf file and add the following line: Storage=persistent /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot. References: 1. https://github.com/konstruktoid/hardening/blob/master/systemd.adoc#etcsystemd journaldconf Additional Information: The main configuration file /etc/systemd/journald.conf is read before any of the custom *.conf files. If there are custom configs present, they override the main configuration parameters
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the journald configuration on Container-Optimized OS requires direct inspection of the `/etc/systemd/journald.conf` file within the guest OS. There is no GCP API, Google Cloud SDK, or GCP-native tool that can natively inspect arbitrary file contents inside a running VM instance without SSH or a custom agent. Therefore, this control cannot be audited through automated GCP-native means.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target Container-Optimized OS instance using the Google Cloud Console or `gcloud compute ssh`. 2. Run the command: `grep -e Storage /etc/systemd/journald.conf` 3. Verify that the output contains the line: `Storage=persistent` and that it is not commented out. 4. Confirm compliance: - If `Storage=persistent` is present and active, the instance is compliant. - If not, the instance is non-compliant and requires remediation.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure permissions on all logfiles are configured
description: Log files stored in /var/log/ contain logged information from many services on the system, or on log hosts others as well.
rationale: It is important to ensure that log files have the correct permissions to ensure that sensitive data is archived and protected.
audit: Run the following command and verify that other has no permissions on any files and group does not have write or execute permissions on any files: # find /var/log -type f -ls
remediation: Run the following commands to set permissions on all existing log files: find /var/log -type f -exec chmod g-wx,o-rwx "{}" + -o -type d -exec chmod g- w,o-rwx "{}" + Additional Information: You may also need to change the configuration for your logging software or services for any logs that had incorrect permissions.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that there is no GCP API, gcloud CLI, or Google Cloud service that can directly enumerate or verify file permissions within the guest OS filesystem (specifically `/var/log`) of running VM instances. This check requires direct OS-level access, which is only possible via SSH into each instance. GCP’s management and monitoring tools do not expose guest file permissions, making automation via GCP APIs impossible.
manual_summary: 1. **Access Each Instance:** - SSH into each Google Container-Optimized OS (COS) VM instance using: ``` gcloud compute ssh <instance-name> --zone=<zone-name> ``` 2. **List Log File Permissions:** - Run the following command to list all log files and their permissions: ``` find /var/log -type f -ls ``` 3. **Verify Permissions:** - For each file, confirm: - The `other` category has no permissions (`---`). - The `group` category does not have write (`w`) or execute (`x`) permissions. - Example of secure permissions: `-rw-r-----` 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - All log files in `/var/log` must have permissions no more permissive than `0640` (owner read/write, group read, others none). 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the permissions for audit evidence. - If any files are non-compliant, remediate with: ``` find /var/log -type f -exec chmod g-wx,o-rwx "{}" + ```

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure logrotate is configured
description: The system includes the capability of rotating log files regularly to avoid filling up the system with logs or making the logs unmanageably large.
rationale: By keeping the log files smaller and more manageable, a system administrator can easily archive these files to another system and spend less time looking through inordinately large log files.
audit: Review /etc/logrotate.conf and /etc/logrotate.d/* and verify logs are rotated according to site policy.
remediation: Edit /etc/logrotate.conf and /etc/logrotate.d/* to ensure logs are rotated according to site policy. /etc is stateless on Container-Optimized OS. Therefore, /etc cannot be used to make these changes persistent across reboots. The steps mentioned above needs to be performed after every boot. Additional Information: If no maxage setting is set for logrotate a situation can occur where logrotate is interrupted and fails to delete rotated logfiles. It is recommended to set this to a value greater than the longest any log file should exist on your system to ensure that any such logfile is removed but standard rotation settings are not overridden.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires manual verification because the configuration files in question (`/etc/logrotate.conf` and `/etc/logrotate.d/*`) reside within the guest OS of the VM and are not accessible via any GCP-native API, SDK, or CLI. GCP does not provide a mechanism to remotely audit or validate the contents of these files without direct OS-level access (e.g., SSH). Therefore, automation through GCP tools is not feasible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** - In the GCP Console, navigate to Compute Engine > VM instances. - Locate and select the VM running Container-Optimized OS. - Click the "SSH" button to open a terminal session to the instance. 2. **Review logrotate Configuration:** - Run the following commands to display the logrotate configuration: ```bash cat /etc/logrotate.conf cat /etc/logrotate.d/* ``` - Examine the output to verify that log rotation is configured according to your organization's site policy. Specifically, check for parameters such as rotation frequency, maximum file age (`maxage`), and retention settings. 3. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Ensure that log files are rotated at appropriate intervals (e.g., daily or weekly). - Confirm that a `maxage` setting is present to prevent indefinite retention of rotated logs. - Validate that the configuration aligns with your organization's log management and retention policy. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document your findings, including screenshots or command outputs, to demonstrate that logrotate is properly configured. - Note any deviations from policy and recommend remediation if necessary.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure permissions on /etc/ssh/sshd_config are configured
description: The /etc/ssh/sshd_config file contains configuration specifications for sshd. The command below sets the owner and group of the file to root.
rationale: The /etc/ssh/sshd_config file needs to be protected from unauthorized changes by non-privileged users.
audit: Run the following command and verify Uid and Gid are both 0/root and Access does not grant permissions to group or other: # stat /etc/ssh/sshd_config Access: (0600/-rw-------)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
remediation: Run the following commands to set ownership and permissions on /etc/ssh/sshd_config: # chown root:root /etc/ssh/sshd_config # chmod og-rwx /etc/ssh/sshd_config
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the permissions and ownership of `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` on Container-Optimized OS instances requires direct access to the VM's file system. There are no GCP-native APIs, gcloud commands, or OS Config guest policies that can inspect arbitrary in-guest file permissions or ownership for this file. Therefore, this control cannot be automated using GCP tools and must be performed manually via SSH.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Instance:** Use SSH to connect to each relevant VM instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. **Verify File Permissions:** Run the command: `stat /etc/ssh/sshd_config` Review the output for the following: - `Access: (0600/-rw-------)` (permissions set to read/write for owner only) - `Uid: (    0/    root)` (owner is root) - `Gid: (    0/    root)` (group is root) 3. **Expected Secure Configuration:** The file must have permissions of `0600` and both user and group ownership set to `root`. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** The instance is compliant if all the above conditions are met for `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` on every applicable VM.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure permissions on SSH private host key files are configured
description: An SSH private key is one of two files used in SSH public key authentication. In this authentication method, the possession of the private key is proof of identity. Only a private key that corresponds to a public key will be able to authenticate successfully. The private keys need to be stored and handled carefully, and no copies of the private key should be distributed.
rationale: If an unauthorized user obtains the private SSH host key file, the host could be impersonated
audit: Run the following command and verify Uid is 0/root and Gid is 0/root. Ensure group and other do not have permissions # find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key' -exec stat {} \; File: ‘/etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key’ Size: 1679            Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file Device: ca01h/51713d    Inode: 8628138     Links: 1 Access: (0600/-rw-------)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (  0/root) Access: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.861750616 +0000 Modify: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.861750616 +0000 Change: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.873750616 +0000 Birth: - File: ‘/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key’ Size: 227             Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file Device: ca01h/51713d    Inode: 8631760     Links: 1 Access: (0600/-rw-------)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (  0/root) Access: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.897750616 +0000 Modify: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.897750616 +0000 Change: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.905750616 +0000 Birth: - File: ‘/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key’ Size: 387             Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file Device: ca01h/51713d    Inode: 8631762     Links: 1 Access: (0600/-rw-------)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: ( 0/root) Access: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.945750616 +0000 Modify: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.945750616 +0000 Change: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.957750616 +0000 Birth: -
remediation: Run the following commands to set ownership and permissions on the private SSH host key files # find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key' -exec chown root:root {} \; # find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key' -exec chmod 0600 {} \;
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that GCP APIs, gcloud CLI, and native GCP services do not provide the capability to directly inspect file-level permissions inside the guest OS of Compute Engine VMs (specifically for `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key`). The audit requires direct access to the file system, which can only be achieved by SSHing into each instance. While custom automation could be built using startup scripts or third-party agents, there is no GCP-native, supported, or scalable API-based method to perform this check.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH to the Instance:** Use SSH to log into each Compute Engine instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. **Navigate to SSH Directory:** Run `cd /etc/ssh` to access the SSH configuration directory. 3. **List SSH Private Host Key Files and Check Permissions:** Execute: `find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key' -exec stat {} \;` 4. **Verify Ownership and Permissions:** - Confirm that each file's Uid and Gid are `0/root` (owned by root). - Confirm that file permissions are `0600` (`-rw-------`), meaning only the owner (root) can read/write. 5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Ownership: `root:root` - Permissions: `0600` 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - No group or other user should have access to these files. - Document findings for each instance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure permissions on SSH public host key files are configured
description: An SSH public key is one of two files used in SSH public key authentication. In this authentication method, a public key is a key that can be used for verifying digital signatures generated using a corresponding private key. Only a public key that corresponds to a private key will be able to authenticate successfully.
rationale: If a public host key file is modified by an unauthorized user, the SSH service may be compromised.
audit: Run the following command and verify Access does not grant write or execute permissions to group or other for all returned files # find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key.pub' -exec stat {} \; File: ‘/etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key.pub’ Size: 382             Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file Device: ca01h/51713d    Inode: 8631758     Links: 1 Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root) Access: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.861750616 +0000 Modify: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.861750616 +0000 Change: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.881750616 +0000 Birth: - File: ‘/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key.pub’ Size: 162             Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file Device: ca01h/51713d    Inode: 8631761     Links: 1 Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root) Access: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.897750616 +0000 Modify: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.897750616 +0000 Change: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.917750616 +0000 Birth: - File: ‘/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub’ Size: 82              Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file Device: ca01h/51713d    Inode: 8631763     Links: 1 Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root) Access: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.945750616 +0000 Modify: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.945750616 +0000 Change: 2018-10-22 18:24:56.961750616 +0000 Birth: -
remediation: Run the following commands to set permissions and ownership on the SSH host public key files # find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key.pub' -exec chmod 0644 {} \; #find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key.pub' -exec chown root:root {} \;
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of file permissions and ownership on the guest OS filesystem of a running VM (Container-Optimized OS). GCP-native APIs, SDKs, and CLI tools do not provide access to inspect or audit file-level permissions inside a VM instance. Such checks require either direct SSH access or the use of non-GCP-native configuration management tools running within the instance. Therefore, this control cannot be automated using GCP-native capabilities.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Instance:** - In the GCP Console, navigate to Compute Engine > VM instances. - Locate the instance running Container-Optimized OS. - Click "SSH" to open a terminal session to the instance. 2. **Enumerate SSH Public Host Key Files:** - Run the command: `find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key.pub' -exec stat {} \;` 3. **Verify File Permissions and Ownership:** - For each file listed, ensure: - Permissions are set to `0644` (`-rw-r--r--`). - Owner is `root` (Uid: 0) and group is `root` (Gid: 0). - No group or other users have write or execute permissions. 4. **Confirm Secure Configuration:** - If any file does not meet these criteria, remediate using: `find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key.pub' -exec chmod 0644 {} \;` `find /etc/ssh -xdev -type f -name 'ssh_host_*_key.pub' -exec chown root:root {} \;` 5. **Document Results:** - Record the output and verification steps for audit evidence.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH Protocol is set to 2
description: Older versions of SSH support two different and incompatible protocols: SSH1 and SSH2. SSH1 was the original protocol and was subject to security issues. SSH2 is more advanced and secure.
rationale: SSH v1 suffers from insecurities that do not affect SSH v2.
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # grep ^Protocol /etc/ssh/sshd_config Protocol 2
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: Protocol 2 Additional Information: This command not longer exists in newer versions of SSH. This check is still being included for systems that may be running an older version of SSH. As of openSSH version 7.4 this parameter will not cause an issue when included.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the SSH protocol version is configured at the guest OS level within each Compute Engine instance and is not exposed via any GCP-native API, gcloud command, or Security Command Center capability. GCP does not provide a method to directly inspect `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` remotely or via automation unless custom agents or scripts are deployed, which is outside the scope of GCP-native compliance automation.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the Compute Engine Instance:** - Use the following command to access the instance: ``` gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --project=<PROJECT_ID> --zone=<ZONE> ``` 2. **Check SSH Protocol Configuration:** - Run the following command on the instance: ``` grep ^Protocol /etc/ssh/sshd_config ``` 3. **Verify Output:** - Ensure the output is exactly: ``` Protocol 2 ``` - This confirms that only SSH Protocol 2 is enabled. 4. **Document Compliance:** - Record the output and instance details for audit evidence.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH LogLevel is appropriate
description: INFO level is the basic level that only records login activity of SSH users. In many situations, such as Incident Response, it is important to determine when a particular user was active on a system. The logout record can eliminate those users who disconnected, which helps narrow the field. VERBOSE level specifies that login and logout activity as well as the key fingerprint for any SSH key used for login will be logged. This information is important for SSH key management, especially in legacy environments.
rationale: SSH provides several logging levels with varying amounts of verbosity. DEBUG is specifically not recommended other than strictly for debugging SSH communications since it provides so much data that it is difficult to identify important security information.
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep loglevel LogLevel VERBOSE OR loglevel INFO
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: LogLevel VERBOSE OR LogLevel INFO Default Value: LogLevel INFO References: 1. https://www.ssh.com/ssh/sshd_config/
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that there is no GCP-native API, gcloud command, or GCP service that exposes or validates the contents of the `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` file or the SSH LogLevel setting for Container-Optimized OS instances. The audit requires direct inspection of the OS-level configuration, which is only accessible via SSH into the VM. GCP OS Config and Cloud Logging do not provide the necessary visibility or controls for this specific file or parameter.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target Google Cloud VM instance running Container-Optimized OS: - Use: `gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone=<ZONE>` 2. Open the SSH configuration file for editing or viewing: - Use: `sudo vi /etc/ssh/sshd_config` or `sudo cat /etc/ssh/sshd_config` 3. Search for the `LogLevel` directive in the file: - In `vi`, type `/LogLevel` and press Enter. 4. Verify that the `LogLevel` is set to either `INFO` or `VERBOSE`. - Any other value (e.g., `DEBUG`) or absence of the directive is non-compliant. 5. Confirm compliance: - If the value is `INFO` or `VERBOSE`, the instance is compliant. - If not, remediation is required as per the benchmark.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH X11 forwarding is disabled
description: The X11Forwarding parameter provides the ability to tunnel X11 traffic through the connection to enable remote graphic connections.
rationale: Disable X11 forwarding unless there is an operational requirement to use X11 applications directly. There is a small risk that the remote X11 servers of users who are logged in via SSH with X11 forwarding could be compromised by other users on the X11 server. Note that even if X11 forwarding is disabled, users can always install their own forwarders.
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep x11forwarding X11Forwarding no
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: X11Forwarding no
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that there is no GCP-native API, SDK, or CLI method to directly audit the SSH daemon’s X11 forwarding configuration on Container-Optimized OS instances. The required check (`sshd -T | grep x11forwarding`) must be executed at the OS level within the VM, and GCP does not expose this configuration through any of its management or inventory APIs. Therefore, manual inspection via SSH is the only technically feasible approach.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the VM Instance:** - Use SSH to connect to the GCP VM instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. **Verify SSH Configuration:** - Run the command: `sshd -T | grep x11forwarding` 3. **What to Verify:** - Confirm that the output is exactly: `x11forwarding no` - This ensures that X11 forwarding is disabled as required by the control. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The SSH daemon must have `X11Forwarding no` set in its configuration (typically `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`). 5. **How to Confirm Compliance:** - If the command output is `x11forwarding no`, the instance is compliant. Any other output indicates non-compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH MaxAuthTries is set to 4 or less
description: The MaxAuthTries parameter specifies the maximum number of authentication attempts permitted per connection. When the login failure count reaches half the number, error messages will be written to the journald logs detailing the login failure.
rationale: Setting the MaxAuthTries parameter to a low number will minimize the risk of successful brute force attacks to the SSH server. While the recommended setting is 4, set the number based on site policy.
audit: Run the following command and verify that output MaxAuthTries is 4 or less: # sshd -T | grep maxauthtries MaxAuthTries 4
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: MaxAuthTries 4 Default Value: MaxAuthTries 6
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying the SSH MaxAuthTries parameter on Container Optimized OS instances requires direct inspection of the OS-level configuration, which is not exposed via any GCP API, gcloud CLI, or GCP-native automation tools. GCP APIs do not provide access to the contents of `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` or the runtime SSH daemon configuration, making manual SSH access to each instance necessary for compliance verification.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Instance:** Use SSH to connect to each relevant Container Optimized OS instance: `gcloud compute ssh INSTANCE_NAME --zone=ZONE` 2. **Check SSH Configuration:** Run the following command to display the current MaxAuthTries setting: `sudo sshd -T | grep maxauthtries` 3. **Verify Compliance:** Confirm that the output is `maxauthtries 4` or a lower value. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** The value must be 4 or less to be compliant. 5. **Document Results:** Record the output for audit evidence.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH IgnoreRhosts is enabled
description: The IgnoreRhosts parameter specifies that .rhosts and .shosts files will not be used in RhostsRSAAuthentication or HostbasedAuthentication.
rationale: Setting this parameter forces users to enter a password when authenticating with ssh.
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep ignorerhosts IgnoreRhosts yes
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: IgnoreRhosts yes Default Value: IgnoreRhosts yes
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of an OS-level SSH daemon configuration parameter (`IgnoreRhosts`) on Google Container-Optimized OS instances. There is no GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native service that exposes the contents of `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` or the runtime output of `sshd -T` for direct, remote, read-only inspection. This check requires SSH access to the instance and manual command execution.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the target instance running Google Container-Optimized OS using the Google Cloud Console or your terminal. 2. Run the following command to check the SSH configuration: ``` sshd -T | grep ignorerhosts ``` 3. Verify that the output is exactly: ``` IgnoreRhosts yes ``` This confirms that `.rhosts` and `.shosts` files are not used for authentication. 4. If the output does not match, edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` to set `IgnoreRhosts yes`, then restart the SSH daemon.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH HostbasedAuthentication is disabled
description: The HostbasedAuthentication parameter specifies if authentication is allowed through trusted hosts via the user of .rhosts, or /etc/hosts.equiv, along with successful public key client host authentication. This option only applies to SSH Protocol Version 2.
rationale: Even though the .rhosts files are ineffective if support is disabled in /etc/pam.conf, disabling the ability to use .rhosts files in SSH provides an additional layer of protection.
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep hostbasedauthentication HostbasedAuthentication no
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: HostbasedAuthentication no Default Value: HostbasedAuthentication no
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that this control requires verification of an OS-level SSH configuration parameter (`HostbasedAuthentication`) within the guest OS of GCP Compute Engine instances running Container-Optimized OS. There is currently no GCP API, gcloud command, or GCP-native tool that can directly inspect or report the contents of `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` or the output of `sshd -T` without logging into the instance. Container-Optimized OS also has limited support for OS Config, and does not expose SSH configuration by default.
manual_summary: 1. Use SSH to log into each GCP Compute Engine instance running Container-Optimized OS: - Run: `gcloud compute ssh <INSTANCE_NAME>` 2. Once logged in, verify the SSH configuration by running: - `sudo sshd -T | grep hostbasedauthentication` 3. Confirm that the output is: - `hostbasedauthentication no` 4. If the output does not match, edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` and set `HostbasedAuthentication no`, then restart the SSH service with: - `sudo systemctl restart sshd` 5. Document the result for each instance as evidence of compliance.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH root login is disabled
description: The PermitRootLogin parameter specifies if the root user can log in using ssh. The default is no.
rationale: Disallowing root logins over SSH requires system admins to authenticate using their own individual account, then escalating to root via sudo or su. This in turn limits opportunity for non-repudiation and provides a clear audit trail in the event of a security incident
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep permitrootlogin PermitRootLogin no
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: PermitRootLogin no Default Value: PermitRootLogin without-password
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of the SSH daemon configuration at the operating system level, specifically the `PermitRootLogin` parameter in `sshd_config`. GCP APIs, gcloud, or any GCP-native tool do not expose or allow direct inspection of this OS-level configuration for running VM instances. Therefore, automation via GCP-native tooling is not feasible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into the GCP VM instance running Container-Optimized OS. 2. Run the command: `sshd -T | grep permitrootlogin` 3. Verify that the output is exactly: `permitrootlogin no` 4. If the output matches, the instance is compliant. If not, edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` to set `PermitRootLogin no`, then restart the SSH service. 5. Document the result for audit purposes.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH PermitEmptyPasswords is disabled
description: The PermitEmptyPasswords parameter specifies if the SSH server allows login to accounts with empty password strings.
rationale: Disallowing remote shell access to accounts that have an empty password reduces the probability of unauthorized access to the system
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep permitemptypasswords PermitEmptyPasswords no
remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameter as follows: PermitEmptyPasswords no Default Value: PermitEmptyPasswords no
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires manual verification because the SSH daemon configuration (specifically, the PermitEmptyPasswords parameter) resides within the guest OS and is not exposed via any GCP API, gcloud command, or other GCP-native automation tools. GCP does not provide a method to remotely query or audit the contents of /etc/ssh/sshd_config or the effective SSH configuration on a VM instance without direct OS-level access.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the VM Instance:** - Use SSH to securely connect to the target VM instance in Google Cloud. 2. **Check SSH Configuration:** - Run the following command on the VM: `sshd -T | grep permitemptypasswords` 3. **Verify Output:** - Ensure the output is: `permittEmptyPasswords no` - This confirms that the SSH server does not allow logins with empty passwords. 4. **Remediate if Necessary:** - If the output is not as above, edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` and set `PermitEmptyPasswords no`, then restart the SSH service.

source: CIS GOOGLE CONTAINER OPTIMIZED OS BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure SSH PermitUserEnvironment is disabled

rationale: Permitting users the ability to set environment variables through the SSH daemon could potentially allow users to bypass security controls (e.g. setting an execution path that has ssh executing trojan'd programs)
audit: Run the following command and verify that output matches: # sshd -T | grep permituserenvironment PermitUserEnvironment no
